topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red ink on the paper seemed to mock me. A C-minus. After weeks of grueling effort, late nights fueled by lukewarm coffee and sheer stubbornness, this was the reward? I stared at the grade as if it were a physical assault. My jaw tightened, and I slowly crumpled the sheet, the sound sharp in the otherwise silent study room. I didn't bother to look at the professor’s feedback; it was just another tedious list of corrections I was supposed to incorporate into my next, equally doomed attempt. A slow, simmering resentment began to coil in my chest, a heavy, unpleasant weight. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum echoing my internal frustration. "Predictable," I muttered under my breath, the word tasting like ash.

[story 2]

I picked up the graded essay with two fingers, turning it over under the harsh fluorescent light. The score—a disheartening 68%. My stomach clenched, a tight knot of sour anxiety. I felt a sudden, hot flush rise up my neck, and I instinctively pulled my collar higher around my throat, a small, defensive gesture. Why? Because it was an insult. I reread the comments, each sentence a tiny barb aimed directly at my perceived inadequacy. I didn't raise my voice when I spoke to my roommate later; instead, I just slammed my textbook shut with unnecessary force, the sound loud enough to make him jump. "Well," I said, my tone flat and devoid of warmth, "that confirms what I already suspected."

[story 3]

The notification flashed across my screen, stark and unforgiving: 72%. I blinked several times, trying to process the sheer absurdity of the outcome. A solid, unremarkable mark that felt like a deliberate slight. I leaned back in my desk chair, the leather groaning in protest beneath my weight. My gaze drifted toward the window, where the afternoon sun illuminated dust motes dancing in the air—a perfect metaphor for the scattered, wasted effort. I picked up my pen, not to start revising, but just to tap the end of it rhythmically against the desk. The tapping was sharp, impatient, a constant reminder of the gulf between what I aimed for and what I had actually achieved.
